1: David addresses the full assembly for the last time. He describes Solomon as naʿar wārāḵ (young and inexperienced) — the same vulnerability formula used of Israel in the wilderness (Numbers 14:29) and of Jeremiah at his call (Jeremiah 1:6) — to explain why the most elaborate preparation is necessary. The bîrāh (citadel-palace) is a Persian loanword that appears only in late biblical literature (Nehemiah 1:1; Esther 1:2; Daniel 8:2), marking 1 Chronicles as a post-exilic composition addressed to a community that knows Persian administrative vocabulary. David has already supplied the incomprehensible quantity of materials (1 Chronicles 22, 28); 1 Chronicles 29 records the people’s matching generosity.
2: Verses 2–5 itemize David’s personal treasury contribution: gold, silver, bronze, iron, timber, onyx, stones for setting, antimony, stones of various colors, marble. The list mirrors the Tabernacle material list (Exodus 25:3–7; 1 Chronicles 35:5–9), deliberately evoking the Mosaic precedent. As Israel brought freewill offerings to build the Tabernacle (Exodus 36:3–7 — so much they had to be restrained), the whole nation will now bring freewill offerings to prepare for the Temple.
3: David distinguishes his sᵉgullātî (personal treasure) from the official temple treasury he has already dedicated (1 Chronicles 22, 28). Sᵉgullāh carries the connotation of a king’s private hoard, his most cherished possessions — the same word used of Israel as God’s treasured possession (Exodus 19:5; Deuteronomy 7:6; Psalms 135:4). David is now giving his most personal wealth over and above his official donation, modelling the principle of giving from one’s own abundance rather than only from institutional resources.
4: The quantities are staggering by any ancient standard: 3,000 talents of gold (Ophir gold, the finest) and 7,000 talents of refined silver. Scholars note these numbers are theologized — they signal completeness and abundance rather than precise historical accounting. The Chronicler’s audience would recognize the genre: as Tabernacle building required lavish supply, so Temple preparation surpasses it. Ophir (v4) was the legendary gold source (1 Kings 9:28; 1 Chronicles 10:11; Job 22:24; Psalms 45:9) — the best gold available.
5: Millēʾ yāḏ — “to fill the hand” — is the technical term for priestly ordination throughout the Torah (Exodus 28:41; 1 Chronicles 29:9; Leviticus 8:33; Numbers 3:3). David uses it here for lay giving: who then will volunteer to consecrate himself today to the LORD? The priestly ordination language transforms the act of giving into an act of self-consecration. Generous giving is priestly service; the worshiper who gives lavishly participates in the Temple’s sacrificial system as surely as the priest who serves at its altar.
6: The śārîm (leaders/princes), the tribal officers, the commanders of thousands and hundreds, and the royal overseers (śārê ʿăḇôdat hammelek) all respond with spontaneous freewill giving. The verb hitnaddēḇ (reflexive stem of nādaḇ) appears 4x in vv6–9 — it means “gave willingly/voluntarily.” The Chronicler uses it to echo the Tabernacle narrative (Exodus 35:21, 29) and to distinguish this offering from required tithes or taxes. The leaders set the pattern for the people.
7: The Chronicler records the offering in multiple currency denominations: gold dārkᵉmônîm (darics), silver talents, bronze talents, and iron talents. The daric was a Persian gold coin minted from the reign of Darius I (522–486 BC), making this an anachronism in David’s time — another post-exilic marker. The Chronicler is translating ancient wealth into terms his post-exilic audience would recognize, just as modern translations might render ancient sums in contemporary currency. The total — 5,000 talents and 10,000 darics of gold — signals overwhelming abundance.
8: Precious stones are entrusted to Jehiel the Gershonite for the temple treasury. The explicit naming of the treasury custodian reflects the Chronicler’s priestly accountability concerns — the same concern that runs through the gatekeeping and treasury records of 1 Chronicles 9 and 26. Temple wealth required responsible stewardship, a concern that resonated strongly with post-exilic communities that had experienced the plundering of the First Temple.
9: The people rejoiced (wayyiśmᵉḥû haʿām) because they had offered willingly (bᵉlēḇ šālēm — with a whole/undivided heart). David also rejoiced greatly (wayyiśmaḥ dāwîd hammelek śimḥāh gᵉḏôlāh). The Chronicler inserts joy and wholehearted devotion as the measure of proper worship — the same lēḇāḇ šālēm (whole heart) that is the Chronicler’s signature phrase for covenantal faithfulness (1 Chronicles 16:10; 1 Chronicles 22:19; 1 Chronicles 28:9; 1 Chronicles 29:9, 19). The offering is acceptable because it comes from undivided hearts.
10: David’s prayer (vv10–19) is the theological climax of 1 Chronicles. It begins with the classic Jewish berakah formula: bārûḵ ʾattāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ʾāḇînû mēʿôlām wᵉʿaḏ ʿôlām — “Blessed are you, LORD, God of our father Israel, from everlasting to everlasting.” This exact formula stands behind the synagogue liturgy and the NT doxologies (Luke 1:68; Romans 1:25; 2 Corinthians 11:31). The prayer is addressed to God as the God of the patriarchs — David situates his temple project within the sweep of salvation history from Abraham to Solomon.
11: The great doxology of v11 ascribes five attributes to YHWH: gᵉḏûllāh (greatness), gᵉḇûrāh (power/might), tīpʾāret (glory/splendor), neṣaḥ (victory/perpetuity), and hôḏ (majesty). Everything in heaven and earth belongs to YHWH; sovereignty (mamlaḵāh) belongs to him; he is exalted as head above all. This fivefold attribute list became foundational in Jewish liturgy. The phrase “Yours is the kingdom, the power, and the glory forever” in the doxology appended to the Lord’s Prayer (Matthew 6:13 text tradition) draws directly on this verse — the most explicit NT echo of 1 Chr 29.
12: Verse 12 elaborates the sovereignty of v11: riches and honor come from YHWH; he rules over all; in his hand are power and might; in his hand it lies to make great and give strength to all. The theology here deliberately dismantles any sense that Israel’s wealth — just lavishly displayed in the freewill offering — originates with Israel itself. The Chronicler’s post-exilic community, living under Persian dominion, needed this reminder: material abundance is a gift from the God who rules over all nations and empires.
13: Wᵉʿattāh ʾĕlōhênû môḏîm ʾănaḥnû lāḵ ûmᵉhallᵉlîm lᵉšēm tipʾartᵉḵā — “And now, our God, we give thanks to you and praise your glorious name.” The pivot from declaration (vv10–12) to thanksgiving (v13) is the classic berakah structure: praise → declaration of God’s attributes → expression of gratitude. This is the template for liturgical prayer throughout the Psalter and continues in NT thanksgiving formulas (Philippians 4:6; Colossians 3:17; 1 Thessalonians 5:18).
14: The paradox of giving: kî-mî ʾănî ûmî ʿammî kî-naʿṣôr kōaḥ lᵉhitnaddēḇ kāzōʾt kî-mimmᵉḵā hakkōl ûmiyyāḏᵉḵā nātannû lāḵ — “For who am I, and what is my people, that we should be able thus to offer willingly? For all things come from you, and of your own we have given you.” This is the Chronicler’s most profound theological statement. The paradox — that human giving is only returning what God first gave — became a cornerstone of Christian theology of stewardship. The act of giving is simultaneously an act of acknowledging dependence.
15: Kî-gērîm ʾănaḥnû lᵉpānêḵā wᵉtôšāḇîm kᵉḵol-ʾăḇōtênû kᵉṣēl yāmênû ʿal-hāʾāreṣ wᵉʾên miqweh — “For we are strangers before you and sojourners, as all our fathers were. Our days on the earth are like a shadow, and there is no abiding.” The gēr wᵉtôšāḇ (stranger and sojourner) formula echoes Abraham’s self-description in purchasing the cave of Machpelah (Genesis 23:4) and Psalms 39:12. The post-exilic community, many still in diaspora, heard their own condition described: even in the land, Israel is only a sojourner; the earth is not their permanent home.
16: David rehearses that all the material wealth — the entire massive stockpile — came from YHWH’s hand in the first place. The temple materials are being returned to their ultimate owner. This framing transforms what might look like a lavish royal building project into an act of pure worship: the people are not constructing an impressive monument to themselves; they are dedicating back to God what was always his.
17: Yōḏaʿtî ʾĕlōhay kî ʾattāh bōḥēn lēḇāḇ — “I know, my God, that you test the heart and have pleasure in uprightness.” YHWH as heart-tester is a consistent biblical motif (Psalms 7:9; 1 Chronicles 17:3; Jeremiah 11:20; 1 Chronicles 17:10; 1 Chronicles 20:12). In the Chronicler’s theology, external offerings are meaningless without internal integrity — the whole-heart (lēḇāḇ šālēm) that he has insisted upon throughout. David then confirms that his own giving was done in uprightness (bᵉyōšer) and with a willing heart (ûbᵉnᵉdāḇāh).
18: David asks YHWH to keep the yēṣer maḥšᵉḇôt lēḇāḇ — “the inclination of the thoughts of the heart” — of his people forever directed toward God. Yēṣer is the same word used in Genesis 6:5 for the yēṣer of the human heart that was “only evil continually” before the flood. David prays that the good inclination of Israel’s heart — displayed in this moment of lavish giving — would be preserved permanently. The prayer acknowledges that human devotion is fragile and requires divine sustaining.
19: The final petition for Solomon: wᵉliš lōmōh bᵉnî tēn lēḇāḇ šālēm lišmôr miṣwōtêḵā ʿēḏwōtêḵā wᵉḥuqqêḵā — “Grant to Solomon my son a whole heart to keep your commandments, your testimonies, and your statutes.” The threefold covenant formula (miṣwōt, ʿēḏôt, ḥuqqîm) mirrors Deuteronomy 6:1 and is the Chronicler’s Deuteronomistic signature. Solomon needs the same undivided heart (lēḇāḇ šālēm) that has characterized the ideal Israelite throughout 1 Chronicles — to do all things and build the palace (bîrāh) for which David has made provision.
20: David commands the whole assembly to bless YHWH. All the assembly blessed YHWH and bowed their heads (wayyiqqdû) and prostrated themselves (wayyištaḥăwû) before YHWH and before the king. The double prostration — to YHWH and to the king — is theologically deliberate: the king’s throne is YHWH’s throne (1 Chronicles 29:23). Worship of the Davidic king is not idolatry but participation in the theocracy — the human king is the visible representative of the divine King.
21: The assembly offers burnt offerings (ʿōlôt) the next day: 1,000 bulls, 1,000 rams, 1,000 lambs — the round-number tripling signals completeness and royal scale. The accompanying drink offerings (nᵉsāḵîm) and abundant sacrifices for all Israel complete the festal worship. The feast follows the offering — joy and feasting are the appropriate response to sacrifice accepted. The Chronicler pictures this as a national Eucharist: the people eat and drink in the presence of YHWH with great joy (bᵉśimḥāh gᵉḏôlāh).
22: Solomon is anointed (wayyamšᵉḥû) as king before YHWH — “a second time” (šēnît), the first anointing having been the emergency coronation at Gihon (1 Kings 1:39, which the Chronicler omitted). Zadok is simultaneously anointed as priest (lᵉkōhēn). The dual anointing — king and high priest — at the same ceremony establishes the Solomonic theocracy: the Zadokite priesthood (which will serve the First Temple for nearly 400 years and be revived in Ezekiel’s vision of the restored temple, Ezek 40–48) is formally inaugurated here in the Chronicler’s account.
23: Wayyēšeḇ šᵉlōmōh ʿal-kissēʾ YHWH lᵉmeleḵ taḥat Dāwîḏ āḇîw — “Then Solomon sat on the throne of the LORD as king in place of his father David.” Kissēʾ YHWH — the throne of the LORD — is a phrase unique to 1 Chronicles (1 Chronicles 17:14; 1 Chronicles 28:5; 1 Chronicles 29:23). In Samuel-Kings the phrase is “the throne of Israel” or “the throne of David.” The Chronicler deliberately elevates the Davidic throne to a divine throne, identifying the Davidic monarchy as the visible seat of YHWH’s rule on earth. The post-exilic community, living without a king, hears this as promise: the throne belongs to YHWH and will be restored.
24: All the leaders, the mighty men, and all the sons of David pledged their allegiance (nātᵉnû yāḏ — literally “gave the hand”, a gesture of submission and loyalty) under King Solomon. The inclusion of “all the sons of David” is significant — it means David’s other sons, including Adonijah (whose attempted coup is narrated in 1 Kgs 1), submitted to Solomon. The Chronicler’s streamlined account presents a smooth, uncontested succession — his theological point: God’s chosen king is accepted without division.
25: YHWH made Solomon exceedingly great (waygaddēl YHWH ʾet-šᵉlōmōh lᵉmaʿlāh) before all Israel and bestowed on him royal majesty (hôḏ malḵût) such as no king over Israel had had before him. The hôḏ malḵût formula echoes the doxology of v11 (hôḏ) — Solomon’s royal majesty is a gift of the same divine majesty that David praised. The Chronicler presents Solomon’s greatness not as achieved but as bestowed — God glorifies his chosen king as part of establishing his own kingdom.
26: Verse 26 begins David’s death notice. David son of Jesse reigned over all Israel — the summary formula that closes major regnal accounts. The statement “all Israel” is the Chronicler’s consistent emphasis: David ruled the unified nation, not just Judah. The earlier years in Hebron ruling Judah only (2 Sam 2–4) are not mentioned by the Chronicler; for his purposes, David’s reign is always pan-Israelite.
27: David’s regnal summary: forty years in total — seven in Hebron, thirty-three in Jerusalem. The identical figures appear in 2 Samuel 5:4–5 and 1 Kings 2:11. The forty-year total is a round number with theological resonance (wilderness wandering, Saul’s reign, Solomon’s reign all forty years) — the Chronicler presents Israel’s ideal rulers as receiving a full generation of reign.
28: David died in a good old age (bᵉśêḇāh ṭôḇāh), full of days (śāḇaʿ yāmîm), riches (ʿōšer), and honor (kāḇôḏ). The bᵉśêḇāh ṭôḇāh / śāḇaʿ yāmîm formula is the patriarchal death formula used for Abraham (Genesis 25:8), Isaac (Genesis 35:29), and Job (Job 42:17). The Chronicler places David firmly in the tradition of the patriarchs: a man who finished well, fully satisfied with life, honored by God and man. No mention of the deathbed political maneuvering of 1 Kgs 1–2.
29: The Chronicler cites three prophetic source documents for David’s reign: the diḇrê Šᵉmûʾēl hārōʾeh (words/acts of Samuel the seer), the diḇrê Nātān hannāḇîʾ (acts of Nathan the prophet), and the diḇrê Gāḏ hāḥōzeh (acts of Gad the seer). All three figures appear in Samuel-Kings; the Chronicler credits them as source authors. This triple citation is the Chronicler’s standard source-formula — he will cite similar prophetic sources for each subsequent king — legitimating his account by appeal to prophetic testimony rather than court annals.
30: The three prophetic sources are said to contain: all of David’s deeds (kol-diḇrê Dāwîḏ hammelek), the first and the last (hārīʾšōnîm wᵉhāʾaḥărōnîm), and all the times/circumstances that passed over him (wᵉhāʿittîm ʾăšer ʿāḇᵉrû ʿālāyw) and over Israel and all the kingdoms of the earth. The comprehensive formula closes David’s account: his story is embedded in universal history — the kingdoms of the earth are the backdrop against which YHWH’s choice of David and establishment of his throne is narrated. This universalist horizon sets up 2 Chronicles’ opening with Solomon’s temple — the place where all nations will come.
